title: Setting up gravity drip irrigation from a water butt
subtitle: Running drip lines or soaker hose from a raised butt without a pump
excerpt: Gravity-fed drip irrigation from a raised water butt delivers water slowly at root level, reducing evaporation and surface-water runoff. It requires the butt to be elevated at least 500 mm above the beds and a simple filter at the outlet.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on drip irrigation in vegetable gardens. Waterwise UK water-efficient irrigation guidance. Garden organic guidance on gravity-fed watering systems.

content.text: Gravity
drip irrigation
from a
water butt

needs the water surface to sit at least 500 mm above the drip emitters. That gives about 0.05 bar of pressure at the emitters. Enough for soaker hose and low-flow drip tape, but not enough for spray heads or rotating sprinklers.
System components
Raised stand: bricks, concrete blocks, or a proprietary water butt stand raising the butt 500-800 mm above the bed. Higher provides more pressure and longer runs.

In-line filter: a simple mesh filter (100-150 micron) at the butt outlet prevents debris blocking drip emitters. Without a filter, algae and organic debris from the butt blocks emitters within a season.

Soaker hose or drip tape: soaker hose is porous and seeps along its full length; drip tape has emitters at fixed spacings (typically 200-400 mm). Soaker hose is simpler for beds; drip tape is better for rows with wider spacings.

Manual timer or gravity timer: a tap timer on the butt outlet controls run duration. Battery-powered timers require at least 1 bar to function; gravity-operated mechanical timers work at any pressure and suit water butt use.
Setting up the system
Step 1, raise the butt.
Position the butt on its raised stand, ensuring it is stable and level. An unstable stand with a full 200-litre butt is a serious hazard; use a solid, level base.
Step 2, fit the filter and timer.
Screw the filter onto the butt tap thread, then the timer (if used) onto the filter outlet. Use PTFE tape on thread connections to prevent drips.
Step 3, run the main supply hose.
Run a 13 mm supply hose from the butt outlet to the bed. Keep runs under 30 m for gravity flow. Cap the end of the supply hose and fit T-connectors or saddle clamps at each row position.
Step 4, lay drip lines or soaker hose.

Push 4 mm drip tube into the saddle clamp connectors. Run soaker hose or drip tape along each row and peg flat with hose pegs. Test by opening the butt tap: each emitter should drip slowly. Adjust the timer for a 30-to-60-minute run per watering cycle in summer.

attrs.body: Soaker hose from a gravity butt delivers approximately 1-3 litres per metre per hour depending on head and hose type. A 5-metre soaker hose on a 60-minute run delivers approximately 5-15 litres: enough for one run of raised-bed crops in dry weather. Run daily or every other day in peak summer. Check by digging 50 mm into the soil beside a plant after a run: the soil should be damp at that depth.
attrs.title: How much water does it deliver?

content.text: The full cycle of
rainwater harvesting
and gravity drip needs no power. No mains tap at the garden. And very little attention once it is set up.
